content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Everyone assumes modern AI models outperform traditional search techniques. Our research found that isn’t always true.
For years, duplicate bug report detection has been considered a natural application for deep learning. With powerful transformer models and sentence embeddings becoming the standard, it’s tempting to believe classical information retrieval techniques like BM25 are obsolete.
I wanted to test that assumption.
Instead of evaluating models on carefully selected pairs of bug reports — as many previous studies do I evaluated them in the way software teams actually work.
When a developer files a new bug report, nobody compares it with just one existing report.The system has to search through hundreds of thousands of previous reports and identify possible duplicates.
That changes everything.
The Experiment
I evaluated four retrieval approaches on 675,791 bug reports collected from three major open-source projects:

• Eclipse

• NetBeans

• OpenOffice
The methods included:

• TF-IDF

• BM25

• MiniLM Sentence Transformer

• CodeBERT
I also studied how these models behaved when transferred across projects and whether different kinds of bug reports affected retrieval quality.
What I Expected
Like many researchers, I expected the sentence transformer to consistently outperform classical lexical search.
It didn’t.
The Surprise
The winner depended entirely on the content of the bug report.
When reports mainly contained natural language, MiniLM performed best.
When reports were filled with stack traces, exception messages, method names, and source code, BM25 consistently outperformed neural embeddings.
This explains why many benchmark results can be misleading.
Average performance hides what’s actually happening underneath.
Why Does This Happen?
Neural sentence embeddings are designed to understand semantic meaning.
They’re excellent when two people describe the same issue differently.Lexical search, however, excels when documents contain rare identifiers.
Stack traces, package names, filenames, exception classes, and method names are exactly the kind of terms BM25 loves.
Those exact matches often matter more than semantic similarity.
Another Interesting Finding
I also tested whether models trained or configured for one software project could be reused on another.
BM25 transferred surprisingly well.
Even when moving across different projects, retrieval quality only dropped modestly.
That means organizations can often reuse lexical retrieval without extensive retraining.
What About CodeBERT?
One of the most surprising findings involved CodeBERT.
Despite being trained specifically on source code and containing far more parameters than MiniLM, it performed much worse as a retrieval model.
The reason is simple.
Being trained on programming languages doesn’t automatically produce good sentence embeddings.
Contrastive similarity training matters much more than model size.
Practical Advice
If your issue tracker mostly contains:

• Plain-language bug reports → use a sentence embedding model.

• Stack traces and code-heavy reports → BM25 remains an excellent choice.

• A mixture of both → combine lexical and neural rankings using Reciprocal Rank Fusion.
There isn’t a universal winner.
The best retrieval method depends on the content you’re searching.
Final Thoughts
One of the biggest lessons from this research is that newer AI models don’t automatically replace classical algorithms.
Sometimes decades-old information retrieval methods still outperform state-of-the-art neural models.
Rather than asking “Which model is best?”, we should ask:
“Which model is best for this kind of data?”
That’s often a much more useful question.
